operating_state: Revenue reached $7.19 billion, declining 13% year-over-year but increasing 19% sequentially, driven by explosive Data Center growth from generative AI and large language model demand using Hopper and Ampere architectures. Gaming revenue fell 38% annually due to macroeconomic slowdown and channel inventory normalization, while Automotive surged from self-driving platforms and AI cockpit solutions. Revenue concentration among cloud service providers and consumer internet companies intensified.
cost_structure: Gross margin compressed to 64.6% from 65.5% year-over-year, reflecting lower Gaming margins partially offset by higher Data Center compute margins as Hopper architecture ramped. Operating expenses decreased 30% annually due to prior year's Arm acquisition termination charge, while current period expenses increased from employee growth and AI cloud services investments. Supply chain costs increased from wafer, component, and logistics premiums driven by inflationary conditions and extended lead times.
material_events: Company launched four AI inference platforms combining full-stack software with Ada, Hopper and Grace Hopper processors for generative AI workloads. NVIDIA introduced AI Foundations for custom large language models and announced partnerships with Google Cloud and ServiceNow for enterprise generative AI solutions. The company transitioned certain operations out of China and Hong Kong following export control restrictions, introducing alternative A800 and H800 chips for the Chinese market.
macro_exposures: U.S. export controls require licenses for A100 and H100 exports to China, Hong Kong, Macau and Russia, creating material revenue exposure for any future chips achieving specified performance thresholds. Supply chain remains concentrated in Asia-Pacific including Taiwan where critical manufacturing partners operate, creating vulnerability to geopolitical tensions and additional trade restrictions.
forward_outlook: Company procured substantially higher Data Center supply for second half of fiscal 2024 compared to first half, anticipating continued generative AI demand growth. Management committed to expanding cloud service provider partnerships and scaling infrastructure to support growing AI workload demands while investing in AI cloud services and standalone software solutions.
uncertainty_profile: Inventory and supply commitments have grown significantly due to supply chain conditions and long lead times, creating potential for provisions if misaligned with demand. Pending export control modifications could subject additional products to licensing requirements, potentially disrupting supply chains and market access. Demand forecasting complexity increased due to cryptocurrency mining volatility, generative AI adoption uncertainty, and gray market resale dynamics.
